Client                          Server
  │                              │
  │── Request (id=1) ───────────│
  │  {"method": "tools/call"}    │
  │                              │

  │  {"result": {...}}           │
  │                              │
: JSON-RPC defines the message format, while MCP defines what the methods
Understanding the internals of protocol messages (json-rpc) helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Production experience with protocol messages (json-rpc) has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Deploying protocol messages (json-rpc) in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.

By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.

Protocol Messages (JSON-RPC) is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.

MCP uses JSON-RPC 2.0 as its wire protocol — a lightweight, stateless remote procedure call protocol that predates MCP by many years and is widely implemented across languages and platforms. Every MCP message is a JSON object that follows one of three shapes: a Request (client sends to server, expects a response), a Response (server replies to a request), or a Notification (one-way message with no response expected). The distinguishing field is the presence and structure of the id field: Requests and Responses carry a matching id (string or integer), while Notifications omit id entirely. All three shapes share the jsonrpc: "2.0" field as a version declaration. The method field on Requests identifies the operation being invoked. Understanding this foundation makes MCP message traces immediately readable without MCP-specific documentation.
jsonrpc: "2.0" is mandatory in every message; it distinguishes JSON-RPC from other JSON protocols.
id: string or integer that links a Request to its Response; must be unique within a session; Notifications omit id.
method: string naming the operation (e.g., "tools/call", "resources/read"); only present in Requests and Notifications.
params: optional object or array of arguments for the method; shape is method-specific.
result: present in successful Responses; shape is method-specific; mutually exclusive with error.
error: present in failed Responses; contains code (integer), message (string), and optional data field.

Every MCP session begins with a capability negotiation handshake that is completed before any tool calls, resource reads, or prompt requests are made. The client sends an initialize Request declaring its protocol version, client name/version, and the set of capabilities it supports (e.g., roots for filesystem access, sampling for model invocation). The server responds with its own protocol version, server name/version, and the capabilities it provides (tools, resources, prompts, logging). The client then sends an initialized Notification to signal that it has processed the server's capabilities and is ready to proceed. This three-message sequence ensures both sides agree on what features are available before any application-level messages are exchanged. MCP clients must refuse to send method calls before receiving the server's initialize Response.
Client sends initialize Request with protocolVersion, clientInfo, and capabilities object.
Server replies with its own protocolVersion, serverInfo, and the capabilities it can serve.
Client sends initialized Notification (no id) to complete the handshake.
Protocol version mismatch: if client and server disagree on version, the server should return an error and close the connection.
Capabilities are additive: a server that supports both tools and resources declares both in one capabilities object.
The initialize exchange must complete before any tools/call, resources/read, or prompts/get requests are sent.

Tool invocation in MCP uses the tools/call method. The client sends a tools/call Request with the tool name and a JSON object of arguments; the server executes the tool and replies with a Response containing the result or an error. The result shape is a content array where each item has a type field: "text" for plain text output, "image" for base64-encoded image data, or "resource" for a URI reference. Multiple content items allow a tool to return a mix of text explanation and image output in a single response. Error handling has two layers: a JSON-RPC error Response (for protocol-level failures like unknown method) and a content result with isError: true (for tool-execution failures like file not found). Claude should treat isError: true results as tool failures and explain the error to the user rather than silently ignoring it.
Image result: content item with type="image", data=base64 string, mimeType="image/png".
tools/list Request retrieves the full list of available tools with their names, descriptions, and input schemas.

Resources in MCP represent readable data sources — files, database records, API responses, or any content a server exposes for Claude to read. The resources/list method returns a paginated list of available resources with their URIs, names, MIME types, and descriptions. The resources/read method fetches the content of a specific resource identified by URI. Resources can be text (returned as a string) or binary (returned as base64-encoded data with a MIME type). Some servers implement resource templates: URI patterns with variable segments (e.g., file:///{path}) that clients can expand with specific values. Servers that support live data can implement resources/subscribe and resources/unsubscribe, pushing notifications when a resource changes, though this capability is optional and must be declared in the initialize handshake.
resources/list: returns [{uri, name, description, mimeType}] with optional nextCursor for pagination.
resources/read: params contain uri; result contains contents array with text or blob items.
Resource templates: URIs with {variable} segments allow parameterized reads without listing every possible URI.
resources/subscribe: server pushes notifications/resources/updated when subscribed resource content changes.

MCP errors follow the JSON-RPC 2.0 error object specification with additional MCP-defined error codes. The error object contains three fields: code (a negative integer identifying the error category), message (a human-readable English description), and an optional data field for machine-readable error details. Standard JSON-RPC codes include -32700 (Parse error), -32600 (Invalid Request), -32601 (Method not found), -32602 (Invalid params), and -32603 (Internal error). MCP reserves codes from -32099 to -32000 for implementation-specific errors such as resource not found, access denied, or rate limited. Clients should handle errors gracefully by checking the stop_reason from Claude's response: if tool_use was the stop reason and the tool result contains isError: true, Claude can be informed of the failure and asked to retry or suggest alternatives.
-32700 Parse error: the server received a message that is not valid JSON.
-32601 Method not found: client called a method the server does not implement (e.g., resources/read when server has no resources).
-32602 Invalid params: required parameter is missing or has wrong type — check the method's input schema.
-32603 Internal error: server-side exception during method execution; check server logs for details.
Implementation errors (-32000 to -32099): server-defined codes for application-level errors like auth failures.
Claude behavior on tool error: treat isError:true results as failures; the model should report the error to the user, not silently retry.
